summary: Spolsky expresses excitement about Scott Rosenberg's forthcoming book 'Dreaming in Code,' which chronicles the troubled development of Chandler, an open-source personal information manager. He praises Rosenberg as the journalist who best understands software development, citing a previous interview experience. Spolsky notes the irony that Chandler was hyped as an 'Outlook killer' in 2003 but four years later had only reached version 0.7alpha4. The post also briefly touches on the ethics of accepting review copies as a blogger.
key_points: Scott Rosenberg stands out among technology journalists for his deep understanding of software development
Chandler's development serves as a cautionary tale: hyped as an 'Outlook killer' in 2003, it was still in early alpha four years later
Being hyped by the press early in development is 'almost certainly a bad sign' for a software project
The question of why software projects fail or stall remains fascinating and worth studying through narrative journalism
money_quotes: Of all the journalists covering technology and especially software development I think Scott understands it the best.
I've been watching Chandler closely ever since it was probably doomed by being breathlessly hailed by Wired Magazine as 'The Outlook Killer' in January, 2003.
Well, it's January 2007 now, Outlook isn't dead, and Chandler is up to 0.7alpha4.
Being hyped by Wired Magazine is almost certainly a bad sign.
I've been wondering what the heck went so wrong on the Chandler team, or perhaps it's just a matter of good software taking ten years?
key_insight: Early media hype is a warning sign for software projects, and understanding why ambitious projects like Chandler stall is one of the most important questions in software development.
